title: Why Taste Beats AI in the Age of Infinite Output
excerpt: As AI democratizes production, judgment becomes the bottleneck. Taste—knowing why something works—is the skill no model can replicate, and it's more valuable than ever.
author: AIToolCrunch
content:
Taste Still Matters: Why Judgment Is the Skill AI Cannot Replace

When everything becomes producible on demand, the constraint shifts. It's no longer about who can generate - it's about who knows what's worth generating. Taste has never been more valuable.

The economics of this are worth spelling out clearly. A decade ago, the bottleneck in creative work was production capacity. You could have great taste but if you lacked the technical skills or resources to execute, your judgment stayed locked in your head. Now those constraints have dissolved. Midjourney can turn a paragraph into a polished image. Cursor can turn a requirement into working code. The tools don't care if you're building something important or frivolous - they produce either way.

This reversal creates an opportunity for anyone willing to think about it differently.

Taste versus preference

Start with the definition because it matters. Taste is not preference. You can prefer chocolate to vanilla without having taste - preference is just a personal inclination. Taste is knowing why one thing works better than another, being able to articulate that reasoning, and being right about it consistently enough that people trust your judgment.

A photographer with taste doesn't just pick the best photo from a contact sheet. They understand the light, the timing, the composition - what the frame is communicating that the alternatives aren't. They can point to specific choices and explain why they worked. That understanding is what lets them make better photos next time. Without it, they're just lucky when things turn out well.

A developer with taste reads a pull request and doesn't just say "this looks elegant." They identify what makes code legible to the next person who has to modify it, what patterns will cause maintenance headaches in year two, where clarity beats cleverness. That judgment is what makes their code reviews valuable, not just their ability to spot syntax errors.

Here's what AI cannot do: it cannot have taste in this sense. It generates outputs that match statistical patterns in its training data, and those outputs are often good - sometimes genuinely excellent. But the model has no understanding of why an output is good. It has no concept of the problem it's solving or the user it's solving it for. It knows what resembles things that got positive responses. That's different from understanding.

Where this plays out right now

Image generation is the clearest example because it's so visible. Tools like Ideogram and Leonardo AI can now produce images that would have required days of skilled work five years ago. The technical bottleneck has evaporated.

But here's what actually changed: two people using the same tool with the same subject get radically different results. The person with taste writes prompts that encode specific visual decisions. Lighting direction. Aspect ratio. The mood they want the color grading to suggest. What should be sharp and what should fade into the background. Every choice serves the image.

The person without taste describes the subject and hopes. Maybe it works. Usually it's closer to generic.

The tool doesn't compensate for vague direction by making smart aesthetic choices. It makes choices, but they're determined by statistical likelihood, not intent. The taste has to live in the prompter. The model can't supply what it doesn't understand.

In coding, the pattern is identical. Tools like Cursor or Claude can write substantial code from a description. The description determines everything. A developer with taste specifies the actual constraints: this needs to be readable by someone six months from now, this is hot-path code so clarity beats cleverness, this is a prototype so get it working before polishing it. Those constraints are taste expressed as requirements.

A developer without taste says "write me a function that does X" and uses the first output. A developer with taste knows what good looks like for this specific context and iterates until they get there.

The person with taste understands not just what the output should look like, but why. The tool cannot provide that understanding - it can only provide outputs.

The uncomfortable part

Taste is learnable but it is not shortcuttable. You cannot use AI to develop the judgment to know which outputs are good. You can use AI to generate more things faster, but that just makes the judgment problem larger, not smaller.

Taste develops through the same unglamorous path it always has: exposure, feedback, time. You see many examples of something. You develop intuitions about what works and why. You make things. You get responses. Your intuitions get refined. The process is slow because there are no shortcuts.

What AI changes is that it removes the natural constraints that used to force the development of judgment. Photographers used to develop taste partly because film and processing cost money - you couldn't afford to shoot carelessly, so you learned to be intentional. Now you can generate thousands of images in an afternoon. The forcing function is gone. The only way to develop judgment is to impose it deliberately: to decide, for each output, why it works or why it doesn't, and to keep refining your understanding.

Developers who use AI coding tools extensively without doing this will find that their intuitions about good architecture and maintainable code atrophy. The tool produces more code faster, but the judgment about what the code should actually do, how it should be organized, and what matters for long-term viability still has to come from somewhere. If you're not exercising that judgment muscle, it gets weaker.

What this means in practice

If taste is the scarce resource in an AI-saturated environment, treat it as one. Be deliberate about what you consume and why. Read criticism - of design, of writing, of code - to build vocabulary for understanding why things work. Make things yourself without AI occasionally, to keep the judgment sharp. Review AI output actively instead of accepting it passively.

Be honest about which tasks require taste and which don't. Generating boilerplate. Summarizing documents. Producing first drafts of structured content. These are good uses of AI where taste isn't the bottleneck. The final edit of anything that will be seen or used by others - that still needs a person with developed judgment.

The market is catching up to the fact that AI democratizes production. It does not democratize taste. The people who understood this early, who treated judgment as the skill worth developing while the tools handled execution, are going to be disproportionately valuable as the technology spreads.

Production has been getting cheaper for years. Judgment is about to become the thing everyone actually pays for.
